core_story.problem: Introduction Humans perceive a scene as a collection of objects [ 35].

core_story.summary: Introduction Humans perceive a scene as a collection of objects [ 35]. However, numerous promising slot-attention methods [45, 64, 65] have remained limited to synthetic and simple datasets [ 16, 26, 33, 37]. *Project page and code: https://visinf.github.io/glass/ Figure 1. Abstract Object-centric learning aims to decompose an input im- age into a set of meaningful object files (slots). To address this, we introduce Guided Latent Slot Diffusion (GLASS), a novel slot-attention model that learns in the space of generated images and uses semantic and instance guidance modules to learn better slot embeddings for various downstream tasks. Our experiments show that GLASS surpasses state-of-the- art slot-attention methods by a wide margin on tasks such as (zero-shot) object discovery and conditional image gen- eration for real-world scenes.

introduction_structure.main_message: Introduction Humans perceive a scene as a collection of objects [ 35].
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Transitions from problem framing to the high-level solution.
introduction_structure.evidence_text:
1. Introduction
Humans perceive a scene as a collection of objects [ 35].
Such a decomposition of the scene into objects makes hu-
mans capable of higher cognitive tasks like control, reason-
ing, and the ability to generalize to unseen experiences [25].
Building on these ideas, object-centric learning (OCL) aims
to decompose a scene into compositional and modular sym-
bolic components. OCL methods bind these components
to latent (neural) representations, enabling such models to
be applied to tasks like causal inference [61], reasoning [3],
control [5], and out-of-distribution generalization [15].
Slot-attention models [45], a popular class of OCL meth-
ods, decompose an image into a set of latent representations
where each element, called slot, competes to represent a
certain part of the image. Slot-attention methods can be cat-
egorized as form of representation learning, where the repre-
sentation (slots) facilitates various downstream tasks such as
property prediction [15], image reconstruction [32], image
editing [73], and object discovery [62]. However, numerous
promising slot-attention methods [45, 64, 65] have remained
limited to synthetic and simple datasets [ 16, 26, 33,

introduction_structure.evidence_text:
constructing the encoder features. GLASS relies on two key
observations: (1) Learning in the space of images generated
using diffusion models allows to generalize well to real im-
ages because the distribution of the generated images closely
mimics the real data distribution [ 23, 60, 66, 68]; and (2)
learning with generated images allows us to use a pre-trained
diffusion model, such as Stable Diffusion [56], as a pseudo
ground-truth generation engine. To this end, GLASS relies
on a semantic guidance module, which uses the diffusion de-
coder to generate the pseudo-semantic mask. The semantic
guidance module helps GLASS address over-segmentation
issues and obtain precise boundaries.
However, using semantic guidance alone biases the slots
to semantic classes instead of instances in an image, causing
under-segmentation. To resolve this issue, we use an instance
module in the form of an MLP decoder similar to [62], which
reconstructs the encoder features to counteract slots drifting
towards semantic classes. This enables the slots to learn
better slot embeddings, which are more instance centric.
While some of GLASS’s modules have been considered in
isolation, its novel combination of

Moreover, the quality of the obtained slot represen- tations remains limited as witnessed by both qualitative and quantitative results, which show the slots to suffer from the issue of over-segmentation (segmenting an object into multi- ple slots), under-segmentation (segmenting multiple objects into one slot), or imprecise object boundaries.
However, using semantic guidance alone biases the slots to semantic classes instead of instances in an image, causing under-segmentation.
method_expression: To address this, we introduce Guided Latent Slot Diffusion (GLASS), a novel slot-attention model that learns in the space of generated images and uses semantic and instance guidance modules to learn better slot embeddings for various downstream tasks.
This helps our method to learn superior slot embeddings for various downstream tasks compared to existing slot-attention methods.
Our method also outperforms SotA OCL methods for (zero-shot) instance-level segmentation (on Ob- ject365 [63] and CLEVRTex [37]).
Our method makes use of semantic and instance guidance in order to learn better instance-centric representations.
Notably, our method is the first OCL approach that enables the compositional generation of complex real-world scenes.
experiment_expression: We clearly outperform previous SotA OCL methods on various tasks: instance-level (zero-shot) object discovery and conditional image generation.

Abstract Object-centric learning aims to decompose an input im- age into a set of meaningful object files (slots).
Building on these ideas, object-centric learning (OCL) aims to decompose a scene into compositional and modular sym- bolic components.
Conclusion We present GLASS, a novel object-centric learning method that learns in the space of generated images from a pre- trained diffusion model.
Except for this watermark, it is identical to the accepted version; the final published version of the proceedings is available on IEEE Xplore.
Experiments Our main goal is to learn better representations of objects, i.e., slot embeddings.
Finally, we show that GLASS outperforms other weakly-supervised SotA OCL models [ 32] that rely on extra information like bounding boxes or knowing the number of objects in a scene.

raw_sections.abstract:
Abstract
Object-centric learning aims to decompose an input im-
age into a set of meaningful object files (slots). These la-
tent object representations enable a variety of downstream
tasks. Yet, object-centric learning struggles on real-world
datasets, which contain multiple objects of complex textures
and shapes in natural everyday scenes. To address this, we
introduce Guided Latent Slot Diffusion (GLASS), a novel
slot-attention model that learns in the space of generated
images and uses semantic and instance guidance modules to
learn better slot embeddings for various downstream tasks.
Our experiments show that GLASS surpasses state-of-the-
art slot-attention methods by a wide margin on tasks such
as (zero-shot) object discovery and conditional image gen-
eration for real-world scenes. Moreover, GLASS enables
the first application of slot attention to the compositional
generation of complex, realistic scenes.*
raw_sections.introduction:
1. Introduction
Humans perceive a scene as a collection of objects [ 35].
Such a decomposition of the scene into objects makes hu-
mans capable of higher cognitive tasks like control, reason-
ing, and the ability to generalize to unseen experiences [25].
Building on these ideas, object-centric learning (OCL) aims
to decompose a scene into compositional and modular sym-
bolic components. OCL methods bind these components
to latent (neural) representations, enabling such models to
be applied to tasks like causal inference [61], reasoning [3],
control [5], and out-of-distribution generalization [15].
Slot-attention models [45], a popular class of OCL meth-
ods, decompose an image into a set of latent representations
where each element, called slot, competes to represent a
certain part of the image. Slot-attention methods can be cat-
egorized as form of representation learning, where the repre-
sentation (slots) facilitates various downstream tasks such as
property prediction [15], image reconstruction [32], image
editing [73], and object discovery [62]. However, numerous
promising slot-attention methods [45, 64, 65] have remained
limited to synthetic and simple datasets [ 16, 26, 33, 37].
*Project page and code: https://visinf.github.io/glass/
Figure 1. (top) High-level architecture of GLASS. GLASS em-
ploys semantic and instance guidance modules to generate a se-
mantic guidance signal using the decoder network and an instance
guidance signal using the encoder features. This helps our method
to learn superior slot embeddings for various downstream tasks
compared to existing slot-attention methods. (bottom) GLASS can
perform multiple tasks using the learned slot embeddings, such as
object discovery, compositional generation, conditional generation
(reconstruction), and instance-level property prediction.
Some recent methods [32, 36, 62, 73] use powerful, modern
encoder [8, 51] and decoder networks [56] to scale to com-
plex real-world imagery [21, 44]. Yet, these models remain
restricted to object discovery, lacking the versatility to re-
construct or perform compositional generation of realistic
images. Moreover, the quality of the obtained slot represen-
tations remains limited as witnessed by both qualitative and
quantitative results, which show the slots to suffer from the
issue of over-segmentation (segmenting an object into multi-
ple slots), under-segmentation (segmenting multiple objects
into one slot), or imprecise object boundaries. This over- and
under-segmentation issue is also known as the part-whole
hierarchy ambiguity [29–31].
To overcome the above issues, we proposeGuided Latent
Slot Diffusion (GLASS), a weakly-supervised slot attention-
based model that uses a pre-trained diffusion decoder for
reconstructing the input image and an MLP decoder for re-
This CVPR paper is the Open Access version, provided by the Computer Vision Foundation.
Except for this watermark, it is identical to the accepted version;
the final published version of the proceedings is available on IEEE Xplore.
28673

constructing the encoder features. GLASS relies on two key
observations: (1) Learning in the space of images generated
using diffusion models allows to generalize well to real im-
ages because the distribution of the generated images closely
mimics the real data distribution [ 23, 60, 66, 68]; and (2)
learning with generated images allows us to use a pre-trained
diffusion model, such as Stable Diffusion [56], as a pseudo
ground-truth generation engine. To this end, GLASS relies
on a semantic guidance module, which uses the diffusion de-
coder to generate the pseudo-semantic mask. The semantic
guidance module helps GLASS address over-segmentation
issues and obtain precise boundaries.
However, using semantic guidance alone biases the slots
to semantic classes instead of instances in an image, causing
under-segmentation. To resolve this issue, we use an instance
module in the form of an MLP decoder similar to [62], which
reconstructs the encoder features to counteract slots drifting
towards semantic classes. This enables the slots to learn
better slot embeddings, which are more instance centric.
While some of GLASS’s modules have been considered in
isolation, its novel combination of semantic and instance
guidance modules coupled with a diffusion decoder enable
it to faithfully reconstruct / conditionally generate the input
image. More importantly, GLASS for the first time enables
the compositional generation of complex real-world scenes
with slot-attention methods. Fig. 1 illustrates the high-level
architecture and the downstream tasks our model supports.
Through our experiments, we show that GLASS out-
performs existing state-of-the-art (SotA) OCL methods
[32, 36, 62, 73], significantly improving instance-level object
discovery (ca. +9 % mIoUi on VOC [21] and +5 % mIoUi
on COCO [44]). Our method also outperforms SotA OCL
methods for (zero-shot) instance-level segmentation (on Ob-
ject365 [63] and CLEVRTex [37]). GLASS further estab-
lishes a new SotA FID score among OCL methods for condi-
tional image generation. GLASS also makes compositional
generation possible with slot attention for complex real-
world scenes. Moreover, we find that our approach surpasses
language-based methods [46, 54, 72, 79] for semantic-level
object discovery. Finally, we show that GLASS outperforms
other weakly-supervised SotA OCL models [ 32] that rely
on extra information like bounding boxes or knowing the
number of objects in a scene.
raw_sections.experiments:
5. Experiments
Our main goal is to learn better representations of objects,
i.e., slot embeddings. To assess the effectiveness of the
learned representation, we evaluate on various tasks such
as object discovery, instance-level property prediction, re-
construction, and compositional generation. GLASS uses
generated captions from BLIP-2 [ 43], which is trained on
image-caption pairs largely mined from the web (a small por-
tion comes from the COCO dataset); thus, our model can be
considered weakly supervised. Therefore, we also compare
against other weakly-supervised OCL methods. We further
propose a variant termed GLASS†, which uses ground-truth
class labels associated with the input image instead of the
generated caption to generate and for semantic guidance.
raw_sections.conclusion:
6. Conclusion
We present GLASS, a novel object-centric learning method
that learns in the space of generated images from a pre-
trained diffusion model. Our method makes use of semantic
and instance guidance in order to learn better instance-centric
representations. We clearly outperform previous SotA OCL
methods on various tasks: instance-level (zero-shot) object
discovery and conditional image generation. Our work also
surpasses SotA models that use large-scale pre-trained mod-
els for semantic-level object discovery, and learns better
slot representations for instance-level property prediction
than similarly versatile OCL methods. Notably, our method
is the first OCL approach that enables the compositional
generation of complex real-world scenes.
